title: The Fragile Ceasefire Holds — Barely — As Heatwaves and War Strain Every System
subhead: A week that began with US-Iran reciprocal strikes ended with indirect talks in Doha producing a communication channel but no breakthrough, while the Islamabad MoU's 60-day ceasefire entered its third week under constant strain. Russia unleashed its largest barrage on Kyiv, Ukraine struck deep into St Petersburg, and record heatwaves killed thousands across two continents simultaneously. The US Supreme Court reshaped presidential power, the USMCA expired into annual uncertainty, and Gaza passed 1,000 days of devastation.

sections.heading: US-Iran: From Exchange of Fire to Doha's Narrow Corridor
sections.body:
The week opened with the US and Iran trading strikes for the second consecutive weekend. Iran's IRGC fired missiles and drones at US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain on Saturday; the US said none reached their targets. A Panama-flagged cargo vessel was struck by a drone near Hormuz. By Sunday, Axios reported that both sides had agreed to 'stand down,' and Trump announced on Truth Social that Iran had 'requested a meeting' and that talks would take place in Doha.

What followed was a week of incremental but fragile progress. US envoy Steve Witkoff and senior adviser Jared Kushner flew to Qatar, but Iran's deputy foreign minister Kazem Gharibabadi initially denied that any technical talks were scheduled. By mid-week, mediators from Qatar and Pakistan held separate meetings with both sides, and Qatar's foreign ministry spokesman reported 'positive progress.' A communication channel was established for reporting MoU breaches — the first concrete de-escalation mechanism since the war began 126 days ago.

But the fundamentals remain unresolved. Iran insists on sole control of the Strait of Hormuz, the very dispute that triggered the latest escalation. Vice President JD Vance refused to guarantee Washington would not resume military action before the MoU's 60-day window expires. Iran's parliamentary speaker denied IAEA access to bombed nuclear sites at Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan, restricting inspectors to Bushehr and the Tehran reactor. Part of the $6 billion in frozen Iranian assets will reportedly be used for purchases Tehran requires, but the broader $12 billion package remains contingent on progress that has not yet materialized. The next meeting is scheduled after Khamenei's funeral, which culminates July 9.
sections.whyItMatters: The Doha process has produced a communication channel and procedural gains, but no breakthrough on the core disputes — Hormuz control, nuclear inspections, or a permanent ceasefire. The 60-day clock is running on a negotiation that the 2015 JCPOA took two years to complete. Every incident near Hormuz could collapse the process.

sections.heading: Khamenei's Funeral: Six Days of Mourning and Power Projection
sections.body:
Six-day funeral ceremonies for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei — killed in the opening US-Israeli strikes on February 28 — began July 4 in Tehran and will culminate in his burial in Mashhad on July 9. Iranian authorities are mobilizing for what acting Tehran mayor Alireza Zakani described as 'the largest assembly in the history of the capital,' with businesses closed and official mourning days declared. A procession through Iraq's holy cities of Najaf and Karbala on July 8 is explicitly designed to project Iran's enduring regional influence.

The dual messaging is unmistakable: institutional continuity and revenge. Regime hardliners used the commemorations to project strength, while DW reported that the war has deepened disillusionment among younger Iranians. Former Russian president Dmitry Medvedev, attending the ceremonies, called the Strait of Hormuz Iran's 'nuclear weapon' — a striking endorsement of Iran's most powerful strategic lever from a nominal bystander. Turkiye's Erdogan said Israel must not be allowed to 'dynamite' the US-Iran MoU, reinforcing the regional coalition seeking to preserve the ceasefire.

Meanwhile, Iran's Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters issued a warning that ships using unapproved routes through Hormuz risk being targeted — a reminder that while commercial traffic has rebounded under the MoU's 60-day window, the underlying security dynamic remains volatile. BBC reporters visiting Bandar Abbas documented seized vessels and stranded tankers at the port.
sections.whyItMatters: The funeral concentrates millions in volatile public gatherings during an already fragile ceasefire period. Any incident could reignite escalation. The Hormuz warning and visible military posture around the strait underscore that the Doha process has not resolved the core security question — who controls the world's most important oil chokepoint.

sections.heading: Russia's Escalation Meets Ukraine's Deep Reach
sections.body:
Russia launched its largest missile and drone barrage on Kyiv of the entire war overnight on July 2-3, deploying 74 missiles and 496 drones across several waves lasting more than 11 hours. At least 27 people were killed and 91 injured, with strikes hitting residential buildings, an ambulance station, a scientific institute, a hotel, and a publishing house where approximately 800,000 books were destroyed. Over 52,500 people sheltered in metro stations — the highest number in recent years.

But Ukraine is hitting back with growing reach. Over 50 Ukrainian attacks on Russian oil refineries, depots, and terminals since March have taken roughly a third of Russia's refining capacity offline. Russia's crude oil processing fell to 3.95 million barrels per day in June — down 25 percent year-on-year and the lowest in over two decades. Gasoline production has dropped 17 percent. Fuel rationing has been introduced in over half of Russia's regions, with hours-long queues at gas stations. Putin publicly acknowledged the shortages for the first time this week, calling them 'not critical' — even as 56 regions enforce restrictions and Crimea has only 'a few days' supply' of fuel.

On Friday, Ukraine struck even deeper, hitting a major oil terminal in St Petersburg — one of Russia's largest Baltic Sea export facilities. Governor Alexander Beglov described a 'massive' drone attack on the city. The strike demonstrates that Ukraine can reach Russia's energy infrastructure anywhere in the country. Meanwhile, BBC Russian documented Russia's escalating recruitment of university students into military contracts, with at least 270 educational institutions promoting one-year terms and large payouts. Recruits who signed up for drone operations say they were thrown into frontline assault units instead. Confirmed Russian military deaths have reached 230,407, with GCHQ estimating the true toll at nearly 500,000.
sections.whyItMatters: The war is now a contest between Russia's willingness to escalate bombardment and Ukraine's ability to degrade Russia's economic lifelines deep in its own territory. Putin's acknowledgment of fuel shortages — and the St Petersburg strike — show that Ukraine's deep-strike campaign is imposing costs that Moscow can no longer conceal from its own population.

sections.heading: Supreme Court: Historic Power Expansion With Conservative Limits
sections.body:
The US Supreme Court concluded its term with a sweeping reshaping of presidential power — but not in the direction many expected. In a 6-3 ruling, the court overturned nearly 90 years of precedent by scrapping the 1935 Humphrey's Executor decision, giving presidents broad authority to fire regulators at independent agencies like the FTC, NLRB, and FCC. Chief Justice Roberts wrote that 'subordinates who exercise the president's power are subject to removal by him.' Trump celebrated, calling it a decision that 'greatly increases presidential power at a time when it is most needed.'

Then the court drew sharp lines. In a 5-4 ruling, Roberts and Kavanaugh joined the three liberal justices to block Trump's attempt to remove Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, with Roberts warning of the 'calamities that could arise' if presidents could impose their will on the central bank. Another 5-4 majority (again with Roberts and Barrett crossing the aisle) upheld birthright citizenship under the 14th Amendment, rejecting Trump's executive order. The court also upheld states' ability to count mail-in ballots received after election day — a direct defeat for Trump's midterm strategy. And it declined to hear Trump's appeal of the $5 million E. Jean Carroll defamation judgment.

The net result: the broadest expansion of presidential removal power in American history, but with conservative justices providing the swing votes to constrain Trump on the Fed, elections, and birthright citizenship. As University of Pennsylvania law professor Kate Shaw observed, this is 'the broadest conception of presidential power that we have ever seen' — but it has boundaries, and this court is willing to enforce them.
sections.whyItMatters: The FTC ruling fundamentally reshapes the administrative state, giving presidents far more control over regulatory agencies. But the court's willingness to constrain Trump on the Fed and elections — with conservative justices in the majority — signals that the expanded presidential power has boundaries. The mail-in ballot ruling could directly affect the midterms in over a dozen states.

sections.heading: Heatwaves Kill Thousands Across Two Continents Simultaneously
sections.body:
Two continents experienced record-breaking heat simultaneously this week, killing thousands and exposing the limits of adaptation infrastructure. In Europe, the WHO linked approximately 2,025 excess deaths to the late June heatwave in France alone — a 29 percent increase in mortality during the last week of June. Germany hit 41.7 degrees Celsius, an all-time national record. Poland set its own record at 40.5 degrees. At least 191 million people across Europe experienced temperatures above 35 degrees. Lingwood in Norfolk reached 37.7 degrees, surpassing the UK's previous June record by more than 2 degrees. Scientists said the heatwave would have been 'virtually impossible' without human-caused climate change, which has made extreme night-time temperatures 100 times more likely than two decades ago.

In the United States, 165 million people faced extreme heat warnings over the Fourth of July weekend. Philadelphia's Semiquincentennial Parade was cancelled outright. Washington DC's Great American State Fair on the National Mall was temporarily shut after multiple people were treated for heat-related illnesses, with at least 11 taken by ambulance. New York City hit 100 degrees Fahrenheit on Thursday — its hottest day since 2012. Trump insisted on an outdoor speech, telling reporters he planned to make 'a really long speech just to show that I can do anything.'

Italy placed 22 cities under red heat warnings. Croatia issued alerts for Zagreb, Split, and Dubrovnik. Italian meteorologists predicted another surge around July 5-6. Funeral homes in Paris struggled to cope with the volume of bodies. Prof Ed Hawkins of Reading University said: 'Our heatwaves will get hotter and hotter and hotter until we get to global net zero.' The concurrent events confirmed what climate models have projected: the adaptation deficit is no longer theoretical — it is killing people and shutting down national celebrations in real time.
sections.whyItMatters: Simultaneous extreme heat on two continents is disrupting national celebrations, straining health systems, and confirming climate projections in real time. Grid operators and public health systems are confronting a baseline that has shifted faster than their infrastructure. A second surge predicted for early July means the toll will continue to climb.

sections.heading: Gaza's 1,000 Days and the West Bank's Expanding Footprint
sections.body:
The Gaza war passed a grim milestone this week: 1,000 days of conflict with 90 percent of the strip's housing units destroyed or severely damaged, 80 percent of Gaza's territory under Israeli military control, and 73,066 killed and missing according to Gaza's Government Media Office. The vast majority of the 2.2 million pre-war population remains displaced with no prospect of return. Israeli violations of the October ceasefire have killed 1,066 people and wounded 3,445 since the truce took effect. Netanyahu said in May he had instructed the military to expand control to more than 70 percent of Gaza. Second-phase ceasefire negotiations remain stalled.

Meanwhile, Israel's Security Cabinet approved plans for 13 new settlements in the occupied West Bank — one of the largest single expansions in years. The decision advances deep into Area C, which comprises over 60 percent of the West Bank and was designated under the Oslo Accords for eventual transfer to Palestinian control. Settler violence against Palestinian communities, including systematic attacks on water infrastructure, has intensified alongside the expansion. Rights groups including Amnesty International have described the Lebanon-Israel framework agreement as betraying victims of war crimes.

Trump hinted that Netanyahu may visit the White House as soon as next week — his first visit since the US-Israel war on Iran began. The visit would come amid a situation where Israel now controls roughly 60 percent of Gaza and occupies about 5 percent of Lebanon, with 13 new settlements further eroding the territorial basis for any future Palestinian state.
sections.whyItMatters: A thousand days of war with 90 percent destruction and no post-war plan is not a stalemate — it is the systematic erasure of a populated territory. The 13 new settlements are not incremental; they are a structural change to the map, making the two-state solution harder to imagine in practice with each passing week.

sections.heading: USMCA Collapses Into Annual Uncertainty as Allies Hedge
sections.body:
The July 1 deadline for the US, Canada, and Mexico to renew the USMCA trade pact came and went without agreement. US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said the United States was 'not ready to renew the pact as it is for another 16 years.' The deal underpins nearly $1.6 trillion in annual trilateral trade and remains in effect while negotiations continue, but the three countries now face rolling annual reviews until the pact's 2036 expiry date.

The US is pushing two demands that would fundamentally alter North American supply chains: raising the 75 percent North American content requirement for automobiles even higher, and introducing a requirement that 50 percent of cars be made in the United States — a demand Canadian PM Mark Carney confirmed. Currently, no USMCA country gets a guaranteed production share. Analysts at Coface estimated that only one in five vehicles imported from Mexico and Canada would meet the 50 percent standard. Trump said he was 'not looking to renew' the pact, adding: 'We don't need anything that they have.'

Canada is already hedging. Carney secured a deal with British Columbia to build a pipeline carrying 1 million barrels per day to the Pacific coast — explicitly framed as reducing dependence on US-bound oil exports and giving Canada direct access to Asian markets. The pipeline still faces environmental review and Indigenous consultation, but the political commitment is formalized. Canada's main sticking point remains US tariffs on steel, aluminium, and automobiles; the US wants greater access to Canada's dairy market and the reversal of provincial boycotts on US alcohol. US ambassador Pete Hoekstra said a near-deal was derailed last October after Ontario ran anti-tariff ads on American networks that angered Trump.
sections.whyItMatters: Annual reviews replace a stable 16-year extension with permanent uncertainty for North American supply chains. Canada's pipeline deal is a tangible sign that US allies are restructuring trade around the assumption that Washington is an unreliable partner. The 50 percent US-content requirement, if adopted, would raise car prices at a moment when consumers are already frustrated by costs.

sections.heading: Venezuela's Earthquake Toll Passes 2,200 as Health Crisis Looms
sections.body:
The official death toll from Venezuela's twin magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 earthquakes on June 24 reached at least 2,295 by mid-week, with more than 11,000 injured and tens of thousands still missing. Nearly 800 buildings collapsed, over 2,500 structures were affected, and more than 500 aftershocks have hit the region. A 21-year-old man was pulled alive on Monday after over 100 hours trapped beneath rubble, found by teams from Venezuela, Mexico, and El Salvador. A toddler was rescued on Tuesday after six days.

But the rescue phase is giving way to a worsening health crisis. Thousands of displaced Venezuelans are sleeping in crowded temporary shelters or outside without clean water. Aid workers warn of untreated injuries, diarrhoea outbreaks, and the risk of infectious disease in overcrowded shelters with dismal sanitation. Eugenio Cova, head of trauma at Hospital Jose Gregorio Hernandez in Caracas, said the medical system is moving from complex trauma to infection-driven complications. The UN is procuring 10,000 body bags.

The US has deployed roughly 900 military personnel and 100 State Department staff, repaired the earthquake-damaged runway at Venezuela's main international airport, and pledged over $300 million in assistance — a fraction of the $6.7 billion in estimated damage. About 50 international aid teams have arrived, including from Ecuador and Israel, which do not have diplomatic relations with Venezuela. The dynamic is striking: US military personnel operating on Venezuelan soil in a humanitarian role, alongside a contested political landscape following the US-backed abduction of Maduro earlier this year.
sections.whyItMatters: The death toll is almost certainly still rising, and Venezuela's economic collapse means it cannot rebuild alone. The US military presence for humanitarian aid creates an unusual dynamic between Caracas and Washington — cooperation amid political hostility — that could reshape bilateral relations or generate new friction depending on how long it lasts.

sections.heading: NATO in Ankara, Poland's Warning, and the Widening Security Perimeter
sections.body:
NATO leaders will convene in Ankara on July 7-8 for the alliance's first summit since The Hague in June 2025, where members committed to spending 5 percent of GDP on defense. The summit takes place against a fundamentally altered backdrop: the US-Iran war, Russia's continued escalation in Ukraine, Trump's relocation of troops and capabilities from Europe, and an accelerating European rearmament push. Germany's Friedrich Merz has defended NATO spending targets after Trump called them 'ridiculous.'

Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk warned publicly of 'critical months' ahead, citing US intelligence reports that Russia is planning an armed provocation on Polish territory to test NATO resolve. The warning is notable for its specificity and for being voiced publicly rather than through diplomatic channels — Warsaw wants the alliance's attention fixed on the eastern flank even as the Middle East dominates the agenda. Russia is reportedly signing up university students for frontline assaults, a sign of personnel shortages that paradoxically increase the risk of Moscow seeking asymmetric escalation elsewhere.

The Ankara summit's agenda includes defense and deterrence planning, spending targets and burden-sharing, further assistance to Ukraine, and the future direction of the alliance. Turkey's hosting role is significant — Ankara has played an outsized diplomatic role in the Iran war, and its position as a NATO member bordering the Middle East gives it leverage in shaping the alliance's southern flank posture. The summit will test whether NATO can align on strategy at a moment when the US is pulling capabilities from Europe, European allies are rearming at unprecedented pace, and the alliance faces active conflicts on two fronts.
sections.whyItMatters: A Russian provocation against Poland — a NATO Article 5 state — would force an immediate alliance crisis at the exact moment NATO is trying to align on long-term strategy. Tusk's public warning signals that the eastern flank threat is not hypothetical. The Ankara summit will reveal whether 5 percent spending commitments translate into real capability gains or remain aspirational.

bottomLine: The week's dominant pattern was fragility across every domain. The US-Iran ceasefire survived its second weekend of reciprocal strikes and produced its first tangible mechanism — a communication channel in Doha — but the core disputes over Hormuz control, nuclear inspections, and a permanent deal remain unresolved, with the 60-day clock running. Russia demonstrated its willingness to escalate with the largest barrage on Kyiv of the war, even as Ukraine struck St Petersburg's oil infrastructure and Russia's fuel crisis deepened to the point where Putin had to acknowledge it publicly. The US Supreme Court gave presidents historic new power over independent agencies — then used conservative justices to constrain Trump on the Fed, birthright citizenship, and elections. Record heatwaves killed thousands across two continents simultaneously, shutting down national celebrations and straining grids. The USMCA expired into annual reviews, and Canada locked in a pipeline to redirect oil away from the US. Gaza passed 1,000 days of devastation. Venezuela's earthquake toll surpassed 2,200. And Poland publicly warned of a Russian provocation days before NATO's Ankara summit. Across every theatre — military, legal, climatic, diplomatic — temporary arrangements are being tested by pressures they were never designed to contain.
sources: Al Jazeera, BBC, Reuters, AP, Axios, AFP, DW, Nikkei Asia, Trading Economics, ISW, Bloomberg, Vortexa, CSIS, Defense News, Morgan Stanley, Energy Intelligence, Kpler, Lloyd's List Intelligence, Mediazona, UN Unama, WHO, CBC, Globe and Mail
